abstract: Modern society has long acknowledged the distinct status of children within legal and judicial frameworks. As the law inevitably reflects evolving social values, recent systemic shifts have been driven by an increased awareness of the need to adapt normative and institutional structures to accommodate the specific vulnerabilities of the child. Such protections are paramount within the repressive arms of the state, specifically criminal and misdemeanor law. Adopting the contemporary terminology of juvenile criminal law, this paper examines the trajectory of the "child in conflict with the law." We provide a comprehensive overview of the normative foundations and future perspectives regarding children’s legal standing, analyzing both supranational standards and their implementation within the Croatian national context. Furthermore, the discussion extends beyond the child as an offender to address the critical position of the child as a victim of crime.
